description: The act of acquiring, satisfying, and retaining customers.
Outlines the specifics of the product and how it will be sold, including the channels that will be used to advertise the product.
Philosophies concerning the factors that should go into market planning.
Conducted for the purpose of new product development or product improvement, often concerned with identifying the consumer's unmet needs.
Concerned with dividing markets into distinct groups of buyers on the basis of distinct needs, characteristics, or behaviors.
Proposes that to complete its organizational objectives, an organization should anticipate the needs and wants of potential consumers and satisfy them more effectively than its competitors.

Defines marketing as the activity, set of institutions, and processes for creating, communicating, delivering, and exchanging offerings that have value for customers, clients, partners, and society at large.
Prolific marketing author and educator who has evolved his definition of marketing.
Defines marketing from a customer-centric perspective, focusing on the management process responsible for identifying, anticipating and satisfying customer requirements profitably.
A government agency that advertises on behalf of an entire industry or locality.
Central to marketing, with needs and wants that marketing aims to satisfy.
Target of marketing efforts, whose needs and wants are anticipated and satisfied.
Marketing strategy or content geared towards a business or organization.
Tactics and strategies in which a company promotes its products and services to individual people.
A business model where end consumers create products and services consumed by businesses and organizations.
A market environment where one customer purchases goods from another customer using a third-party business or platform.
Producing as much as possible of a given product or service to achieve cost advantages.
Producing a variety of products to achieve cost advantages.
An approach that focuses on producing as much as possible to achieve economies of scale or scope.
Focuses on selling and promoting existing products rather than developing new ones.
A firm can survive by producing goods that people are willing and able to buy.
The marketing department guides the actions of a company's other departments.
Embraces social responsibility beyond satisfying customers, including societal stakeholders.
An extension of societal marketing focusing on sustainability.
Involves forging a plan for a firm's marketing activities to accomplish specific objectives.
Four broad categories of marketing decisions: product, price, promotion, and place.
Deals with the specifications of goods or services and their relation to end-user needs.
The process of setting a price for a product, including discounts.
Refers to how a product is distributed to the consumer.
Refers to the methods used to promote a product to consumers.
Channels and intermediaries such as wholesalers and retailers who enable customers to access products or services.
Outlets such as Facebook, Instagram, Twitter, Reddit, Pinterest, Snapchat, TikTok, LinkedIn, and YouTube that facilitate two-way communication between companies and their customers.
A modern marketing mix model proposed by Robert F. Lauterborn, focusing on Consumer, Cost, Convenience, and Communication.
All factors (internal, external, direct, or indirect) that affect a firm's marketing decision-making/planning.
A method of assessing a firm's macro-environment, analyzing political, economic, social, technological, legal, and ecological factors.
The larger societal forces that affect the micro-environment, including economic, social, and technological factors.
The environment that includes factors over which a firm holds more control, such as customers, employees, suppliers, and media.
Factors inside the company itself, such as labor, inventory, company policy, logistics, budget, and capital assets.
A model in marketing that stands for Segmentation, Targeting, and Positioning.
Criteria used to gauge the viability of a target market, including Discernable, Accessible, Measurable, and Profitable.
A tool used by marketing managers to gauge the progress of a product through introduction, growth, maturity, and decline stages.
A strategy used by firms to increase sales through various promotional activities.
A specific, defined segment of the market that a product may serve.
